senses.definition: In genetics and microbiology, the inability of an organism to make a particular organic compound it needs to grow, so it must get that compound from outside.
examples: Auxotrophy in the mutant strain means it cannot grow without extra amino acids in the medium.
Researchers use auxotrophy as a marker to identify genetically modified bacteria.
The yeast shows auxotrophy for uracil and needs it added to the culture.

senses.definition: An abbreviation used before numbers to mean the year of the reign of a Roman emperor, or in some contexts an abbreviation for audiovisual or average, depending on the field.
examples: The company invested heavily in AV equipment for the new conference hall.
The AV technician arrived early to set up the microphones and projector.
In this report, AV stands for average value across all samples.

senses.definition: A female given name.
examples: Ava is studying English at university.
We named our daughter Ava after her grandmother.
Ava called to confirm the meeting time.

senses.definition: In Buddhist literature, a type of moral tale or legend that tells of good deeds and their results.
examples: The monk recited an avadana to teach the value of generosity.
Scholars have translated several important avadana stories from Sanskrit.
This avadana links the Buddha's past lives to his present enlightenment.

senses.definition: A small brightly colored finch, common in parts of Asia and kept as a cage bird.
examples: The avadavat is prized for its bright red plumage.
We saw an avadavat among the reeds by the river.
Many avadavats are bred in captivity for the pet trade.

senses.definition: Plural of avadavat, small brightly colored finches often kept as cage birds.
examples: Several avadavats were kept together in a large aviary.
We spotted two avadavats feeding on the grass seeds.
The market was full of cages with avadavats and other small birds.

senses.definition: In Indian religious traditions, a holy person who has given up normal social rules and lives in deep spiritual freedom.
examples: The avadhuta wandered from village to village, owning almost nothing.
Stories about the avadhuta emphasize his complete detachment from worldly concerns.
The text describes the inner state of an avadhuta who sees everything as one.

senses.definition: A type of woolly lemur that lives in Madagascar.
examples: The avahi is active at night and feeds mostly on leaves.
Conservation programs aim to protect the forest home of the avahi.
We were lucky to see an avahi resting in the branches above us.

senses.definition: To be useful or helpful in achieving something.
examples: His apologies did not avail him.
All her hard work availed little in the end.
No amount of money will avail if you do not have good health.

senses.definition: In Indian English, to use or take something that is offered, such as a service or opportunity.
examples: Students can avail this scholarship by applying online.
Many customers availed the discount before it ended.
Employees are encouraged to avail the training programs.

senses.definition: Use, advantage, or help in doing something.
examples: Their efforts were of no avail.
We tried to persuade him, but to little avail.
The new policy was of some avail in reducing costs.

senses.definition: Nonstandard or misspelled form of available.
examples: Tickets are not availabile online yet.
The document is not availabile on the main website.
They said the service would be availabile soon.

senses.definition: Plural of availability; different times or quantities when something or someone is free or can be used.
examples: Please send me your availabilities for next week.
We compared the seat availabilities on several flights.
The manager checked staff availabilities before making the schedule.

senses.definition: The fact that something is present, able to be used, or easy to get.
examples: The availability of clean water is a serious issue in many regions.
We need to check the availability of meeting rooms.
Product availability may change without notice.

senses.definition: The times when someone is free to meet, talk, or work.
examples: Please let me know your availability for an interview.
My availability changes during exam season.
We matched the tutor's availability with the student's schedule.

senses.definition: That you can get, buy, use, or find.
examples: Tickets are available online and at the door.
Fresh fruit is available in the cafeteria every day.
This information is available on the school website.

senses.definition: Free to see or talk to someone, or not busy.
examples: Are you available for a meeting tomorrow morning?
I am not available on Friday afternoon.
Our support team is available 24 hours a day.

senses.definition: The quality of being available or ready to be used.
examples: The availableness of the data improved the quality of the research.
He praised the constant availableness of the online service.
The report discusses the availableness of affordable housing.

senses.definition: Plural of availableness; different degrees or types of being available or ready for use.
examples: They compared the availablenesses of several online platforms.
Different services show different availablenesses during peak hours.
The study measured the availablenesses of resources across regions.

senses.definition: In a way that is available or ready to be used.
examples: The information is availably presented on the website.
Resources must be availably arranged for quick access.
Funds were not availably distributed to the departments.

senses.definition: Archaic spelling or form of avail, meaning to be of use or help.
examples: His cries did not availe him.
No medicine could availe against the illness.
Her wisdom did not availe in that dark hour.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of avail; was useful or was used.
examples: Their efforts availed nothing.
She availed herself of the chance to study abroad.
No argument availed against his decision.

senses.definition: Someone who makes use of something that is offered.
examples: Every availer of the service must register online.
The scholarship availer must maintain a high grade point average.
Only frequent availers of the program were surveyed.

senses.definition: People who make use of something that is offered.
examples: Availers of the policy must read the terms carefully.
The report lists the main availers of the subsidy.
Regular availers of the service gave positive feedback.

senses.definition: Obsolete spelling of avails, meaning is of use or helps.
examples: He tries, but it availes him nothing.
No prayer availes against their fate.
Her kindness availes but little in a cruel world.

senses.definition: Full of use or benefit; helpful.
examples: Her advice proved highly availful in solving the problem.
It was an availful suggestion at a difficult time.
He made availful contributions to the project.

senses.definition: Present participle of avail; being of use or being used.
examples: Nothing availing, they finally gave up.
She is availing herself of every chance to practice English.
No argument availing, they accepted the decision.

senses.definition: Giving help or benefit.
examples: They made every availing effort to save the company.
Only availing measures should be kept in the plan.
He offered availing support during the crisis.

senses.definition: In a way that gives help or benefit.
examples: She spoke availingly on behalf of the children.
The funds were availingly spent on community projects.
He availingly used his influence to support the cause.

senses.definition: The act of using or taking something that is offered, especially a legal right or benefit.
examples: Availment of tax benefits is subject to certain conditions.
The contract explains the rules for availment of the service.
Improper availment of credit may lead to penalties.

senses.definition: Third person singular of avail; is of use or gives help.
examples: No protest avails against the new law.
Hard work avails little without proper planning.
If honesty avails you, keep speaking the truth.

senses.definition: Profits or financial gains, especially in older or formal use.
examples: The avails of the sale were shared among the partners.
He donated the avails to a local charity.
The company used the avails to pay off its debts.

senses.definition: In finance, a written guarantee to pay a bill or debt if the main debtor does not pay.
examples: The bank provided an aval on the customer's bill of exchange.
Without an aval, the company could not secure the loan.
The aval transfers the risk of non‑payment to the guarantor.

senses.definition: A large mass of snow, ice, or rocks that suddenly falls and moves quickly down the side of a mountain.
examples: The village was destroyed by an avalanche.
Skiers must watch for signs of an avalanche.
A sudden avalanche blocked the mountain road.

senses.definition: A very large amount of things that arrive or happen suddenly at the same time.
examples: The office received an avalanche of complaints.
She faced an avalanche of emails after the conference.
The company was hit by an avalanche of orders.

senses.definition: To fall or come down like an avalanche, or to overwhelm someone with a large amount of something.
examples: Snow avalanched down the mountain after the loud noise.
Boxes avalanched off the shelf onto the floor.
The station was avalanched with calls after the program.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of avalanche; fell like an avalanche or overwhelmed with a sudden large amount of something.
examples: The loose snow avalanched after the climbers passed.
The shelf broke and the books avalanched onto the floor.
The company was avalanched with applications after the job ad.

senses.definition: Plural of avalanche; many avalanches of snow, ice, rocks, or other things.
examples: The region is known for frequent avalanches in winter.
Several avalanches blocked the mountain pass.
They study how avalanches form and move.

senses.definition: Third person singular of avalanche; falls like an avalanche or overwhelms with many things.
examples: Snow avalanches down the slope every spring.
Work avalanches onto her desk at the end of the month.
Mail avalanches into their inbox after each campaign.

senses.definition: Present participle of avalanche; falling like an avalanche or overwhelming with many things.
examples: Snow was avalanching down the mountainside.
Boxes came avalanching off the top shelf.
Requests were avalanching in after the announcement.

senses.definition: An old or dialect form meaning to let something fall or to knock something down.
examples: He did avale the old wall with one strong blow.
The storm did avale many branches from the trees.
They avaled the tower stone by stone.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of avale; knocked down or caused to fall.
examples: The soldiers avaled the gate with a heavy ram.
The storm avaled several chimneys in the village.
They had avaled the fortress walls before sunrise.

senses.definition: In chemistry, having no valence or not forming chemical bonds in the usual way.
examples: An avalent atom does not normally form bonds.
The gas is described as chemically inert and avalent.
Some particles in the model are treated as avalent centers.

senses.definition: Third person singular of avale; knocks down or causes to fall, in archaic use.
examples: He avales the old house with a final kick.
The wind avales the weak trees along the road.
Each wave avales another stone from the wall.

senses.definition: Present participle of avale; knocking down or causing to fall, in archaic use.
examples: They were avaling the ancient tower stone by stone.
The storm came avaling roofs and trees alike.
He spent the day avaling the old sheds behind the house.

senses.definition: In Arthurian legends, an island of magic where King Arthur is taken to be healed; sometimes used more generally for an ideal, peaceful place.
examples: In the story, Arthur is carried to Avalon after his final battle.
The island of Avalon is often shown as a place of eternal spring.
The poet compares the distant valley to a modern Avalon.

senses.definition: Having no valves, especially in a biological or medical context.
examples: The vessel is described as avalvular in the medical report.
Avalvular veins may affect how blood flows in the limb.
The condition involves avalvular segments in the circulatory system.

senses.definition: An obsolete form of advance, meaning a move forward or an improvement.
examples: This policy was an avance in the rights of workers.
In that age, any small avance in science was celebrated.
The treaty marked a great avance toward peace.

senses.definition: In Italian contexts, members of an avant‑garde movement in art, literature, or politics.
examples: The avanguardisti challenged traditional artistic styles.
As avanguardisti, they searched for completely new forms of expression.
The group of avanguardisti met in small cafés to discuss their ideas.

senses.definition: A former term for a forced payment or fine, especially one demanded by an official in parts of the Ottoman Empire.
examples: Merchants feared the unexpected avania imposed at the port.
The avania placed a heavy burden on local traders.
Travelers sometimes paid avania to pass safely through the region.

senses.definition: Having no veins, especially in a botanical or anatomical description.
examples: The leaf is almost avanious, with no visible veins.
Some avanious structures are found in simple plant forms.
The tissue appears avanious under the microscope.

senses.definition: Relating to new, unusual, or experimental ideas, especially in art and culture.
examples: The gallery focuses on avant music and performance.
They are known for their avant approach to fashion.
The film's avant style shocked traditional audiences.

senses.definition: A call meaning 'forward' or 'let us go', used in older or literary English.
examples: Avant, knights, to the castle gate!
Avant, and do not fear the enemy.
He cried, 'Avant!' and led the charge.

senses.definition: An older or French‑influenced form of advantage, meaning a benefit or favorable condition.
examples: He gained much avantage from his studies abroad.
In this game, the first point after deuce is called avantage.
They played with the clear avantage of experience.

senses.definition: People who go before others, especially as leaders or members of the front part of a group, in older use.
examples: The avanters of the army were already at the river.
As avanters in the field, they faced the greatest danger.
The avanters moved ahead to secure the bridge.

senses.definition: Using very new, experimental, or surprising ideas in art, music, or literature.
examples: The festival showcases avantgarde films from around the world.
He is known for his avantgarde approach to theatre.
The singer's avantgarde style divided critics.

senses.definition: Artists or works that are part of an experimental or innovative movement.
examples: The avantgarde challenged every rule of classical painting.
She became a leading voice in the avantgarde.
The gallery features the local avantgarde.

senses.definition: An Italian word meaning 'forward' or 'come in', sometimes used in English in literary or stylistic ways.
examples: The director shouted, 'Avanti!' as the actors ran onto the stage.
He knocked on the door and heard a cheerful 'Avanti!' from inside.
The slogan 'Avanti!' was printed on the campaign posters.

senses.definition: Someone who supports or belongs to an avant‑garde movement, especially in art or politics.
examples: The avantist defended every radical experiment in the exhibition.
As an avantist, she opposed all traditional forms.
The group of avantists met to plan their next project.

senses.definition: People who support or belong to an avant‑garde movement.
examples: The avantists of the movement rejected all old rules.
Many avantists gathered at the festival to present new works.
The book describes how the avantists changed modern art.

senses.definition: A leading or forward part of something, such as the front portion of a structure, in rare technical or historical use.
examples: The engineer inspected the avantlay of the dock.
Damage to the avantlay required urgent repairs.
Workers reinforced the avantlay before the storm season.
